improve：Notably, there was a decrease in the proportion of NK cells in the bloodstream in fibrotic mice following IL-10 intervention. 
Specifically, Further analysis of liver NK cell typing revealed that there was a significant increase in the proportion of peripheral NK cells (CNK) characterized by the surface markers (cd49a-cd49b+) and a decrease in the proportion of liver resident NK cells (lrnk) identified by the surface markers (cd49a+cd49b-).

Following IL-10 intervention, a remarkable decrease in the percentage of NK cells in the bloodstream of fibrotic mice was observed. Additionally, a more detailed examination of liver NK cell typing uncovered significant changes in specific populations. Specifically, there was a notable increase in the proportion of peripheral NK cells (CNK) distinguished by the surface markers (cd49a-cd49b+). Conversely, there was a decrease in the percentage of liver resident NK cells (lrnk) which were identified by the surface markers (cd49a+cd49b-).
improve:After IL-10 treatment in fibrotic mice, the number of NK cells in the liver with strong cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion ability significantly increased
Following IL-10 treatment in fibrotic mice, a notable increase was observed in the number of NK cells within the liver that exhibited strong cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion abilities.

improve：In addition, among various chemokines in the liver, including CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5, only CCL5, a chemokine with obvious function of attracting NK cells, was significantly increased in the IL-10 treatment group

Furthermore, among various chemokines present in the liver, including CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5, it was observed that only CCL5, a chemokine known for its distinct ability to attract NK cells, showed a significant increase in the IL-10 treatment group.

improve：Previous studies have provided compelling evidence indicating that IL-10 intervention effectively enhances both the quantity and functionality of NK cells within liver tissue. It is worth noting that the functionality of NK cells is closely associated with their maturity levels, which can be classified into three distinct stages: immature (cd27+cd11b-), developing (cd27+cd11b+), and mature (cd27-cd11b+). In the context of fibrotic mice receiving IL-10 treatment, a significant increase was observed in the population of developing NK cells within the liver. These developing NK cells exhibited remarkable cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion capabilities, indicating the beneficial effects of IL-10 intervention on NK cell functionality.
Intriguingly, the administration of IL-10 intervention in fibrotic mice resulted in a notable reduction in the proportion of NK cells within the bloodstream. Further analysis of liver NK cell subpopulations revealed that the proportion of peripheral classic NK cells (cNK) characterized by the surface markers (cd49a-cd49b+) exhibited a substantial increase. Conversely, there was a significant decrease in the proportion of liver resident NK cells (lrnk) identified by the surface markers (cd49a+cd49b-). Additionally, among various chemokines present in the liver, including CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5, it was observed that only CCL5, a chemokine known for its distinct ability to attract NK cells, showed a significant increase in the IL-10 group, highlighting its potential role in the observed increase of NK cell presence in the liver following IL-10 intervention.These findings suggest a potential role for IL-10 in modulating NK cell distribution and functionality, ultimately contributing to the regulation of the immune response in the liver microenvironment during fibrosis.

Recent studies have presented compelling evidence supporting the effectiveness of IL-10 intervention in enhancing both the quantity and functionality of NK cells within liver tissue. Notably, the functionality of NK cells is closely associated with their maturity levels, which can be classified into three distinct stages: immature (cd27+cd11b-), developing (cd27+cd11b+), and mature (cd27-cd11b+). In the context of fibrotic mice receiving IL-10 treatment, a significant increase was observed in the population of developing NK cells within the liver. These developing NK cells exhibited remarkable cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion capabilities, suggesting the beneficial effects of IL-10 intervention on NK cell functionality.

Interestingly, the administration of IL-10 intervention in fibrotic mice led to a notable decrease in the proportion of NK cells within the bloodstream. Further analysis of liver NK cell subpopulations revealed a substantial increase in the proportion of peripheral classic NK cells (cNK) characterized by the surface markers (cd49a-cd49b+). Conversely, there was a significant decrease in the proportion of liver resident NK cells (lrnk) identified by the surface markers (cd49a+cd49b-). Additionally, among various chemokines present in the liver, including CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5, it was observed that only CCL5, a chemokine acknowledged for its ability to attract NK cells, showed a significant increase in the IL-10 group. This finding highlights the potential role of CCL5 in the observed increase of NK cell presence in the liver following IL-10 intervention, further emphasizing the modulation of NK cell distribution by IL-10.

Overall, these findings suggest a potential role for IL-10 in modulating the distribution and functionality of NK cells, ultimately contributing to the regulation of the immune response in the liver microenvironment during fibrosis.

Give this paragraph a title：Previous studies have provided compelling evidence indicating that IL-10 intervention effectively enhances both the quantity and functionality of NK cells within the liver tissue. It is noteworthy that the functionality of NK cells is closely associated with their maturity levels, which can be classified into three distinct stages: immature (cd27+cd11b-), developing (cd27+cd11b+), and mature (cd27-cd11b+). In the context of fibrotic mice receiving IL-10 treatment, a significant increase was observed in the population of developing NK cells within the liver. These developing NK cells exhibited remarkable cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion capabilities, indicating the beneficial effects of IL-10 intervention on NK cell functionality. Interestingly, the administration of IL-10 intervention in fibrotic mice resulted in a notable reduction in the proportion of NK cells circulating in the bloodstream. Further analysis of liver NK cell subpopulations revealed that the proportion of peripheral classic NK cells (cNK) characterized by the surface markers (cd49a-cd49b+) exhibited a substantial increase. Conversely, there was a significant decrease in the proportion of liver resident NK cells (lrnk) identified by the surface markers (cd49a+cd49b-). Moreover, among several chemokines detected in the liver, including CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5, it was observed that only CCL5, a chemokine known for its specific ability to attract NK cells, showed a significant increase in the IL-10 group, emphasizing its potential role in the observed increase in NK cell presence in the liver following IL-10 intervention. These findings collectively suggest a crucial role for IL-10 in modulating both the distribution and functionality of NK cells, ultimately contributing to the regulation of the immune response within the liver microenvironment during fibrosis. Further investigation is warranted to fully elucidate the underlying mechanisms and potential therapeutic applications of IL-10 in the context of liver fibrosis.
Title: The Impact of IL-10 Intervention on NK Cell Quantity and Functionality in Liver Fibrosis

Give this paragraph a title：Previous studies have provided compelling evidence indicating that IL-10 intervention effectively enhances both the quantity and functionality of NK cells within the liver tissue. It is noteworthy that the functionality of NK cells is closely associated with their maturity levels, which can be classified into three distinct stages: immature (cd27+cd11b-), developing (cd27+cd11b+), and mature (cd27-cd11b+). In the context of fibrotic mice receiving IL-10 treatment, a significant increase was observed in the population of developing NK cells within the liver. These developing NK cells exhibited remarkable cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion capabilities, indicating the beneficial effects of IL-10 intervention on NK cell functionality. Interestingly, the administration of IL-10 intervention in fibrotic mice resulted in a notable reduction in the proportion of NK cells circulating in the bloodstream. Further analysis of liver NK cell subpopulations revealed that the proportion of peripheral classic NK cells (cNK) characterized by the surface markers (cd49a-cd49b+) exhibited a substantial increase. Conversely, there was a significant decrease in the proportion of liver resident NK cells (lrnk) identified by the surface markers (cd49a+cd49b-). Moreover, among several chemokines detected in the liver, including CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5, it was observed that only CCL5, a chemokine known for its specific ability to attract NK cells, showed a significant increase in the IL-10 group, emphasizing its potential role in the observed increase in NK cell presence in the liver following IL-10 intervention. These findings collectively suggest a crucial role for IL-10 in modulating both the distribution and functionality of NK cells, ultimately contributing to the regulation of the immune response within the liver microenvironment during fibrosis. Further investigation is warranted to fully elucidate the underlying mechanisms and potential therapeutic applications of IL-10 in the context of liver fibrosis.
Title: IL-10 Intervention Enhances NK Cell Quantity and Functionality in Liver Fibrosis

Summarize this paragraph in short sentences：Previous studies have provided compelling evidence indicating that IL-10 intervention effectively enhances both the quantity and functionality of NK cells within the liver tissue. It is noteworthy that the functionality of NK cells is closely associated with their maturity levels, which can be classified into three distinct stages: immature (cd27+cd11b-), developing (cd27+cd11b+), and mature (cd27-cd11b+). In the context of fibrotic mice receiving IL-10 treatment, a significant increase was observed in the population of developing NK cells within the liver. These developing NK cells exhibited remarkable cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion capabilities, indicating the beneficial effects of IL-10 intervention on NK cell functionality. Interestingly, the administration of IL-10 intervention in fibrotic mice resulted in a notable reduction in the proportion of NK cells circulating in the bloodstream. Further analysis of liver NK cell subpopulations revealed that the proportion of peripheral classic NK cells (cNK) characterized by the surface markers (cd49a-cd49b+) exhibited a substantial increase. Conversely, there was a significant decrease in the proportion of liver resident NK cells (lrnk) identified by the surface markers (cd49a+cd49b-). Moreover, among several chemokines detected in the liver, including CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5, it was observed that only CCL5, a chemokine known for its specific ability to attract NK cells, showed a significant increase in the IL-10 group, emphasizing its potential role in the observed increase in NK cell presence in the liver following IL-10 intervention. These findings collectively suggest a crucial role for IL-10 in modulating both the distribution and functionality of NK cells, ultimately contributing to the regulation of the immune response within the liver microenvironment during fibrosis. Further investigation is warranted to fully elucidate the underlying mechanisms and potential therapeutic applications of IL-10 in the context of liver fibrosis.

IL-10 intervention enhances quantity and functionality of liver NK cells. Developing NK cells exhibit cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion abilities after IL-10 treatment. Circulating NK cells decrease, while peripheral classic NK cells increase in the liver. IL-10 treatment leads to an increase in CCL5, attracting NK cells. IL-10 modulates NK cell distribution and functionality in liver fibrosis. Further research is needed for understanding IL-10's mechanisms and therapeutic implications.

improve：Moreover, among several chemokines detected in the liver, including chemokine C-C motif ligand 2, C-C motif ligand 3 and C-C motif ligand 5 (CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5), it was observed that only CCL5, a chemokine known for its specific ability to promote the development and migration of NK cells, showed a significant increase in the IL-10 group, emphasizing its potential role in the observed increase in NK cell accumulation in the liver following IL-10 gene intervention.

Additionally, among the various chemokines detected in the liver such as CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5, it was found that only CCL5, which is known for its specific ability to facilitate the development and migration of NK cells, exhibited a significant increase in the IL-10 group. This finding highlights the potential involvement of CCL5 in the observed enhancement of NK cell accumulation within the liver following IL-10 gene intervention.

improve：Figure 3. NK cell depletion antagonized the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10. ASGM1 was used to deplete NK cells in IL-10-treated fibrotic mice. IL-10 treatment combined with NK cell depletion group (IL-10/ASGM1) were intraperitoneally injected with 20μl of ASGM1 solution per mouse, twice a week starting from the third week, while the remaining mice were injected with rabbit IgG as an isotype control. A verifies the effect of ASGM1 in depleting NK cells in mice liver. B shows histopathological damage in mouse liver. C shows collagen deposition in mouse liver. D shows area analysis of positive collagen fibers in liver stained with Sirius-Red. E shows ALT and AST contents in mice serum. F shows HYP contents in mice liver. Groups: Fibrosis control group (FIBROSIS), IL-10 treatment group (IL-10), IL-10 treatment combined with NK cell depletion group (IL-10/ASGM1). Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 5-10, *p <0.05, **p <0.01, ***p <0.001.

Title: NK Cell Depletion Antagonizes the Anti-Fibrotic Effect of IL-10

Figure 3. NK cell depletion using ASGM1 in IL-10-treated fibrotic mice. Group IL-10/ASGM1 received ASGM1 solution twice a week to deplete NK cells. Control group received rabbit IgG as an isotype control. (A) shows successful depletion of NK cells in the liver. (B) demonstrates histopathological damage. (C) shows collagen deposition. (D) displays the analysis of collagen fiber area using Sirius-Red staining. (E) presents ALT and AST contents in serum. (F) exhibits HYP content in the liver. Fibrosis control group (FIBROSIS), IL-10 treatment group (IL-10), IL-10 treatment combined with NK cell depletion group (IL-10/ASGM1). Data are mean ± SD, n = 5-10, *p <0.05, **p <0.01, ***p <0.001.
